intro.snapshot_description: Simple Harmonic Motion is the cornerstone of oscillation theory — it appears in springs, pendulums, molecules, AC circuits and sound waves. You will master the restoring-force definition (F = −kx), displacement/velocity/acceleration equations, energy inter-conversion between KE and PE, time-period formulae for spring and simple pendulum, spring combinations (series/parallel), special pendulums, and damped/forced oscillations with resonance. Every NEET Biology, Chemistry and Physics paper has at least 3-4 questions rooted directly in this chapter.

expected_questions.description: NEET consistently yields 3-4 SHM questions per paper, typically one on v = ω√(A²−x²), one on T = 2π√(m/k) or T = 2π√(l/g), one energy question, and one conceptual on phase or resonance.

time_required.description: Theory + derivations: ~3 hrs; displacement/velocity/acceleration/energy numericals: ~3 hrs; pendulum and spring combination problems: ~2 hrs; MCQ revision: ~2 hrs.

difficulty_level.description: Formulae are straightforward once the core set (x, v, a, E as functions of displacement) is internalised. Traps arise in phase analysis, spring combination, and pendulum variations (lift, electric field, liquid medium).

intro.most_asked_style: Numerical — given amplitude A, angular frequency ω and position x, find velocity or acceleration. Also frequent: 'time period of spring combination' and 'time period of pendulum in a lift'.
intro.biggest_trap: Confusing velocity and acceleration maxima positions — velocity is maximum at x = 0 (mean position) while acceleration is maximum at x = ±A (extreme positions); students often swap these.
intro.fast_win: Memorise v = ω√(A²−x²) and a = −ω²x together: one gives velocity at any position, the other gives acceleration. These two formulae answer ~50% of all SHM numericals.
intro.revision_friendly: The single energy-position graph (PE = ½mω²x², KE = ½mω²(A²−x²), TE = ½mω²A² = constant) is a one-diagram revision of the entire energy section. Add the table of values at x = 0, x = ±A, x = ±A/2, x = ±A/√2 for instant MCQ recall.

subtopics.subtitle: From pendulums to springs — master the mathematics and physics of oscillation
subtopics.revision_tip: Memorise the three kinematic equations in SHM — displacement x = a sinωt, velocity v = aω cosωt = ω√(a²−x²), acceleration A = −ω²x — then derive all energy, time-period and phase facts from them. Never rote-learn values; always trace them back to these three.

topics.topic_description: Periodic vs oscillatory vs harmonic motion, the defining characteristic of SHM (restoring force ∝ −displacement), angular SHM via restoring torque, and the key terminologies: time period, frequency, angular frequency and phase.

subtopics.name: Periodic and Oscillatory Motion
subtopics.description: Periodic motion repeats after regular interval T. Oscillatory motion: body moves to-and-fro about a fixed mean position. All SHM is periodic but not all periodic motion is SHM.

subtopics.name: Harmonic vs Non-Harmonic Oscillation
subtopics.description: Harmonic: expressible as single sine/cosine, y = a sinωt. Non-harmonic: combination of two or more harmonics, y = a sinωt + b sin2ωt.

subtopics.name: Restoring Force and SHM Definition
subtopics.description: F ∝ −x ⇒ F = −kx. Force constant k: SI unit N/m, dimension [MT⁻²]. Angular SHM: τ ∝ −θ. Time period independent of amplitude.

subtopics.name: Time Period, Frequency and Angular Frequency
subtopics.description: T = least time interval for repetition (SI: second). Frequency n = oscillations per second (SI: Hz). Angular frequency ω = 2πn (rad/s).

subtopics.name: Phase and Initial Phase
subtopics.description: Phase θ = ωt + φ₀ completely describes position and direction at any instant. φ₀ = initial phase (epoch). Same phase: Δφ = 2nπ; opposite phase: Δφ = (2n+1)π.

topics.topic_name: Displacement, Velocity and Acceleration in SHM
topics.topic_description: Mathematical equations for displacement as projection of uniform circular motion, velocity as time-derivative of displacement with its ellipse graph vs displacement, acceleration as proportional to −displacement with its straight-line graph, and phase relationships between the three.

subtopics.name: Displacement Equation
subtopics.description: y = a sinωt (from mean), y = a cosωt (from extreme), y = a sin(ωt ± φ) (general). SHM as projection of UCM on a diameter.

subtopics.name: Velocity in SHM
subtopics.description: v = aω cosωt = ω√(a²−y²). Max at y = 0: v_max = aω. Zero at y = ±a. Graph of v vs y is an ellipse (circle for ω = 1).

subtopics.name: Acceleration in SHM
subtopics.description: A = −ω²y. Max at y = ±a: A_max = ω²a. Zero at y = 0. Graph A vs y is a straight line with slope −ω².

subtopics.name: Phase Relationships
subtopics.description: Velocity leads displacement by π/2; acceleration leads velocity by π/2; acceleration is π ahead of displacement. All three vary with same period.

subtopics.name: Comparative Table at Key Positions
subtopics.description: At mean (y=0): v = max, a = 0, F = 0. At extreme (y=±a): v = 0, a = max = ω²a, F = max = mω²a.

topics.topic_description: Potential energy, kinetic energy and total mechanical energy as functions of displacement and time. Average values over a complete cycle, and energy-position and energy-time graphs.

subtopics.name: Potential Energy
subtopics.description: U = ½ky² = ½mω²y². Max at y = ±a: U_max = ½mω²a² = E. Zero at y = 0. Varies with double frequency of SHM.

subtopics.name: Kinetic Energy
subtopics.description: K = ½mv² = ½mω²(a²−y²). Max at y = 0: K_max = ½mω²a² = E. Zero at y = ±a. Varies with double frequency.

subtopics.name: Total Mechanical Energy
subtopics.description: E = K + U = ½mω²a² = constant (independent of position). TE ∝ a² and ∝ ω².

subtopics.name: Average Energies
subtopics.description: K_avg = U_avg = ½E = ¼mω²a². Equal average values — energy splits equally between KE and PE over a full cycle.

topics.topic_description: Derivation of T = 2π√(l/g), independence of period from mass and amplitude (for small θ), effective length, and the effect of various factors (amplitude, mass, length, g, temperature, liquid medium, electric field) on the time period. Second's pendulum and special pendulum types.

subtopics.name: Derivation of Time Period
subtopics.description: τ = −mgl sinθ ≈ −mglθ for small θ. α = −(g/l)θ ⇒ ω² = g/l ⇒ T = 2π√(l/g). Valid for small amplitude (sinθ ≈ θ).

subtopics.name: Factors Affecting Time Period
subtopics.description: Amplitude: independent (for small oscillations). Mass: independent. Length l: T ∝ √l (effective l = suspension to CM). g: T ∝ 1/√g — slower on hills and in mines, faster on dense planets.

subtopics.name: Temperature Effect
subtopics.description: Rising temperature increases l (thermal expansion) ⇒ T increases ⇒ clock runs slow. ΔT/T ≈ ½ α Δθ.

subtopics.name: Pendulum in a Lift
subtopics.description: Lift at rest or uniform velocity: T = 2π√(l/g). Lift accelerating up: g_eff = g+a ⇒ T decreases (faster). Lift accelerating down: g_eff = g−a ⇒ T increases (slower). Free fall (a=g): T = ∞, no oscillation.

subtopics.name: Pendulum in Liquid / Electric Field
subtopics.description: In liquid (density σ < ρ): g_eff = g(1−σ/ρ) < g ⇒ T increases. In upward electric field (charge q): g_eff = g − qE/m ⇒ T increases. Downward E: g_eff = g + qE/m ⇒ T decreases. Horizontal E: g_eff = √(g² + (qE/m)²).

subtopics.name: Second's Pendulum and Special Types
subtopics.description: Second's pendulum: T = 2 s, l ≈ 99.3 cm (≈ 1 m on Earth). Infinite-length pendulum: T_max ≈ 84.6 min. Compound pendulum: T = 2π√(I/mgl) = 2π√((K²/l + l)/g). Torsional pendulum: T = 2π√(I/C).

topics.topic_description: Spring constant properties, spring pendulum time period, massive spring, reduced mass, and the rules for springs in series and parallel — their effective spring constants and resulting time periods.

subtopics.name: Spring Constant Properties
subtopics.description: k ∝ 1/length. If spring is cut into n equal parts: each part has constant nk. Spring constant dimensional formula [MT⁻²]. k depends on radius, length and material of wire in spring.

subtopics.name: Spring Pendulum Time Period
subtopics.description: T = 2π√(m/k); f = (1/2π)√(k/m). T ∝ √m, T ∝ 1/√k. Independent of g — spring clock keeps correct time on moon, in satellite, inside liquid (if damping neglected).

subtopics.name: Massive Spring and Reduced Mass
subtopics.description: Massive spring (mass M): m_eff = m + M/3. Reduced mass for two masses: 1/m_r = 1/m₁ + 1/m₂.

subtopics.name: Series Combination
subtopics.description: Equal forces act, different extensions. 1/k_s = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂ ⇒ k_s = k₁k₂/(k₁+k₂). T_series = 2π√(m(k₁+k₂)/k₁k₂) = √(T₁²+T₂²).

subtopics.name: Parallel Combination
subtopics.description: Different forces act, equal displacement. k_p = k₁ + k₂. T_parallel = 2π√(m/(k₁+k₂)) = T₁T₂/√(T₁²+T₂²). T_parallel < T_series (stiffer effective spring).

topics.topic_description: Free oscillations (natural frequency), exponentially decaying amplitude in damped oscillations, forced oscillations under an external periodic force, and resonance — when driving frequency equals natural frequency resulting in maximum amplitude.

subtopics.name: Free Oscillations
subtopics.description: Oscillation at natural frequency under restoring force alone. Amplitude, frequency and energy remain constant.

subtopics.name: Damped Oscillations
subtopics.description: Amplitude decreases exponentially: x = x_m e^(−bt/2m) sin(ω't + φ). Damped ω' = √(ω₀² − (b/2m)²). Energy: E = ½Kx_m² e^(−bt/m).

subtopics.name: Forced Oscillations
subtopics.description: External driving force F(t) = F₀cosω_d t. Amplitude: x₀ = (F₀/m)/√((ω₀²−ω_d²)² + (bω_d/m)²). Oscillator frequency locks to ω_d.

subtopics.name: Resonance
subtopics.description: When ω_d = ω₀: amplitude and power absorption are maximum. Sharpness depends on damping. Energy resonance: at ω_d = ω₀, driven oscillator absorbs maximum KE; velocity is in phase with driving force.

subtopics.name: Lissajous Figures
subtopics.description: Two perpendicular SHMs → resultant paths: straight line (φ=0°), ellipse, or circle (φ=90°, a₁=a₂). General equation: x²/a₁² + y²/a₂² − 2xy cosφ/(a₁a₂) = sin²φ.

topics.topic_description: Establishing what SHM is and the language used to describe it — restoring force, time period, frequency, angular frequency and phase.
topics.notes_condensed_description: SHM: F = −kx; k in N/m, [MT⁻²]. Periodic motion repeats after T. Oscillatory: to-and-fro about mean. SHM is periodic but not vice versa. Harmonic: single sine/cosine; non-harmonic: sum thereof. ω = 2πn (rad/s). Phase = ωt + φ₀; φ₀ = epoch. Same phase: Δφ = 2nπ (or path diff = nλ). Opposite phase: Δφ = (2n+1)π. Time period independent of amplitude in SHM.
topics.how_to_revise: Write the definition of SHM in one formula (F = −kx) and state the three consequences: (i) acceleration ∝ −displacement, (ii) time period independent of amplitude, (iii) motion expressible as sine/cosine. Do 5 identification MCQs on 'which of the following is SHM?'

expected_questions.description: Conceptual — identification of SHM from description, or phase difference calculation.

time_required.description: 30 min theory + 30 min MCQs on phase and SHM identification.

difficulty.description: Definitional — no calculation. Traps only arise in same- vs opposite-phase questions.

topics.scoring_focus: All SHM is periodic but not all periodic is SHM. Same phase: Δφ = even multiple of π; opposite: odd multiple.
topics.high_risk_area: Confusing 'same phase' (Δφ = 2π, path diff = λ) with 'in step' vs 'opposite phase' (Δφ = π, path diff = λ/2).
topics.priority_rule: Cover first — all subsequent topics build on the language here.

topics.topic_name: Displacement, Velocity and Acceleration in SHM
topics.topic_description: The three kinematic quantities as functions of time and position, with their extreme values, phase relationships and graphical features.
topics.notes_condensed_description: x = a sinωt (from mean) or a cosωt (from extreme). v = aω cosωt = ω√(a²−x²). At x=0: v_max = aω; at x=±a: v = 0. a = −ω²x. At x=±a: a_max = ω²a; at x=0: a = 0. Velocity leads displacement by π/2; acceleration leads velocity by π/2; acceleration is π ahead of displacement. v-x graph: ellipse (v²/(aω)² + x²/a² = 1), circle if ω=1. a-x graph: straight line, slope = −ω².
topics.how_to_revise: Start from x = a sinωt, differentiate once for v, twice for a. Then verify all extreme values and phase leads. Practise 10 numericals: given x (or position) find v and a. Draw both the ellipse (v vs x) and the straight line (a vs x) from memory.

expected_questions.description: Numerical: given A, ω (or T), find v at a specific x; or find x when v is a given fraction of v_max. One of the most common SHM question types in NEET.

time_required.description: 45 min derivations + 1.25 hrs numericals (at least 15 problems).

difficulty.description: Formula substitution is straightforward; traps are in choosing sin vs cos form and forgetting to take square root of x² term.

topics.scoring_focus: v = ω√(A²−x²) is the single most tested formula in SHM. Internalise it so it can be applied in 10 seconds.
topics.high_risk_area: Swapping max velocity (at mean) and max acceleration (at extreme) positions. Also: forgetting a = −ω²x while writing magnitude |a| = ω²x.
topics.best_practice_style: Direct substitution numericals; graph-interpretation questions asking where KE or velocity is maximum.
topics.priority_rule: Highest priority — this topic alone accounts for ~30% of all SHM exam marks.

topics.topic_description: Potential and kinetic energy as functions of position; total energy as a constant; average values and special positions where KE = PE.
topics.notes_condensed_description: PE = ½mω²y². KE = ½mω²(a²−y²). TE = ½mω²a² = constant. Both KE and PE vary at double the SHM frequency. At y = ±a/√2: KE = PE = E/2. At y = ±a/2: PE = E/4, KE = 3E/4. Average KE = Average PE = E/2 = ¼mω²a². TE ∝ a² (doubles if amplitude √2 times). TE ∝ ω² (doubles if ω √2 times).
topics.how_to_revise: Draw the energy-position graph from memory (inverted parabola for KE, upright parabola for PE, horizontal line for TE). Mark x = 0, ±a/2, ±a/√2, ±a on x-axis with the exact energy fractions. Solve 8 energy MCQs.

expected_questions.description: One energy question per NEET paper is standard: 'KE equals PE at what displacement?', 'energy stored when amplitude changes', etc.

time_required.description: 45 min notes + 45 min problems on energy at specific positions.

difficulty.description: Formulae are simple; the trap is in the average energy question and the double-frequency of PE/KE.

topics.scoring_focus: KE = PE at y = a/√2 (not a/2). Average KE = Average PE = E/2. TE ∝ a².
topics.high_risk_area: Students write 'KE = PE at y = a/2' — the correct answer is y = a/√2. Also: energy varies at 2ω not ω.
topics.best_practice_style: Graph-reading MCQs; calculation of displacement when given KE = (3/4)TE or similar.
topics.priority_rule: High priority — energy questions are almost guaranteed in NEET SHM.

topics.topic_description: Time period derivation, factors that change or do not change T, and special pendulum situations tested in NEET.
topics.notes_condensed_description: T = 2π√(l/g); independent of mass and amplitude (for small θ). T ∝ √l: girl stands → l decreases → T decreases. T ∝ 1/√g: higher altitude/mine → g decreases → T increases → clock slows. Temperature rise: l = l₀(1+αΔθ) → ΔT/T ≈ ½αΔθ. Lift: g_eff = g±a; free-fall: T = ∞. Liquid (σ<ρ): g_eff = g(1−σ/ρ) → T increases. Second's pendulum: T = 2 s, l ≈ 99.3 cm. Infinite length: T_max ≈ 84.6 min. Compound: T = 2π√(I/mgl).
topics.how_to_revise: Make a one-page table: Situation → g_eff → T change → clock behavior (fast/slow). Cover lift (4 cases), altitude, temperature, liquid, electric field. Practice 10 pendulum MCQs.

expected_questions.description: One pendulum question per NEET — most commonly on lift scenario or g-change effect on clock behavior ('clock runs fast/slow').

time_required.description: 1 hr reading the factor table + 1 hr solving scenario-based MCQs.

difficulty.description: Derivation is standard; the difficulty is in the many special cases (lift, electric field, liquid medium).

topics.scoring_focus: Pendulum in lift: 4 cases (rest/uniform, accelerating up, accelerating down, free-fall). Clock behavior: if T increases → clock runs slow.
topics.high_risk_area: Reversing behavior when lift accelerates down vs free-falls. Also: forgetting that mass independence extends even to hollow spheres filled with water.
topics.best_practice_style: Situation-based MCQs: 'A pendulum is taken to the moon — what happens to its time period?'
topics.priority_rule: High priority — pendulum appears in nearly every NEET paper.

topics.topic_description: Spring constant rules, spring pendulum time period, and the critical formula rules for series and parallel spring combinations.
topics.notes_condensed_description: k ∝ 1/length → halve spring → k doubles. Spring cut into n parts: each part = nk. n parts in parallel: k_eff = n²k, T = T₀/n. Spring T = 2π√(m/k); independent of g. Massive spring: m_eff = m + M/3. Reduced mass for two-body system: 1/m_r = 1/m₁ + 1/m₂. Series: 1/k_s = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂; T_series = √(T₁²+T₂²). Parallel: k_p = k₁+k₂; T_parallel = T₁T₂/√(T₁²+T₂²). T_parallel < T_series always.
topics.how_to_revise: Do at least 10 spring combination problems — including a spring-cut problem and a two-mass reduced-mass problem. Verify T_series > T_parallel with a numerical example (k₁ = k₂ = k gives T_series = T√2, T_parallel = T/√2).

expected_questions.description: Spring combination or spring-cut problem appears in most NEET physics papers. Two masses on a spring is an emerging trend.

time_required.description: 30 min spring rules + 1 hr problems on combinations and cutting.

difficulty.description: Series/parallel rules are analogous to resistors (parallel) and capacitors (series) — remember that the analogy is reversed.

topics.scoring_focus: T_series = √(T₁²+T₂²) is frequently directly tested. Also: spring constant of each piece when spring is cut.
topics.high_risk_area: Using capacitor analogy for springs — spring series formula looks like resistor parallel (1/k_s = Σ1/kᵢ), not like capacitor series.
topics.best_practice_style: Computation MCQs with two specific spring constants; also spring-cutting problems.
topics.priority_rule: High priority — appears in most NEET papers and is directly calculable.

topics.topic_description: Qualitative and semi-quantitative understanding of how oscillations lose energy, respond to external driving forces, and achieve maximum amplitude at resonance.
topics.notes_condensed_description: Free oscillation: natural frequency, constant amplitude. Damped: amplitude x = x_m e^(−bt/2m), energy E = ½Kx_m² e^(−bt/m). Forced: amplitude x₀ = (F₀/m)/√((ω₀²−ω_d²)²+(bω_d/m)²). Resonance: ω_d = ω₀ → maximum amplitude (finite due to damping), maximum power absorption. At resonance: velocity in phase with driving force. Maintained oscillation compensates energy loss from external source. Lissajous figures: two perpendicular SHMs; φ=0→line, φ=π/2 & a₁=a₂→circle, general→ellipse.
topics.how_to_revise: Draw the resonance curve (amplitude vs ω_d) showing peak at ω₀ for different damping values. Note: larger damping → broader, lower peak. Memorise the four oscillation types (free/damped/forced/maintained) with one-line definitions.

expected_questions.description: Conceptual questions on resonance conditions, or identification of oscillation type. Sometimes a Lissajous figure shape question.

difficulty.description: Mostly conceptual — no complex numericals on damped oscillations in NEET.

topics.scoring_focus: Resonance condition (ω_d = ω₀), effect of damping on resonance amplitude, Lissajous figures shapes.
topics.high_risk_area: Confusing 'amplitude resonance' (maximum displacement) with 'energy/velocity resonance' (maximum power absorption, velocity in phase with force).
topics.best_practice_style: Conceptual MCQs and assertion-based questions on damping effects.
topics.priority_rule: Medium priority — one conceptual question possible; skip numerical details of damping equations.

mistakes.label: Max v at extreme, max a at mean
mistakes.description: Students swap the two: velocity is maximum at the mean position (x=0) where acceleration = 0, and acceleration is maximum at the extreme (x=±A) where velocity = 0.

mistakes.label: Both max at same point
mistakes.description: Some students think v and a peak at the same instant — they actually peak a quarter-cycle (T/4) apart.

topics.example: In a spring-mass system with A = 10 cm and ω = 5 rad/s: at x = 0, v = 50 cm/s (max) and a = 0; at x = 10 cm, v = 0 and a = 250 cm/s² (max).
topics.trap_framing: NEET questions often ask 'at what position is kinetic energy maximum?' or 'where is restoring force zero?' — both answers are mean position, but students pick extreme position.

trap_question.stem: A particle executing SHM has amplitude A and angular frequency ω. The acceleration of the particle is maximum at:
trap_question.trick: a = −ω²x: acceleration is maximum when x is maximum, i.e., at the extreme position x = ±A. Velocity is maximum at x = 0.

topics.quick_rule: v_max at x = 0 (mean); a_max at x = ±A (extreme). KE max at mean; PE max at extreme. They are π/2 out of phase with each other.

mistakes.label: KE = PE at y = A/2
mistakes.description: The most common wrong answer — students halve the amplitude. The correct answer is y = A/√2 ≈ 0.707A.

mistakes.label: Setting KE = ½E instead of KE = PE
mistakes.description: Correct approach: ½mω²(A²−y²) = ½mω²y² ⇒ A² − y² = y² ⇒ y = A/√2.

topics.example: If A = 10 cm: KE = PE when y = 10/√2 ≈ 7.07 cm, NOT at y = 5 cm.
topics.trap_framing: NEET options typically include both A/2 and A/√2 — the A/2 option is placed first to trap students who guess by symmetry.

trap_question.stem: At what displacement from the mean position is the kinetic energy equal to the potential energy in SHM?
trap_question.trick: Set KE = PE: ½mω²(A²−y²) = ½mω²y² ⇒ y² = A²/2 ⇒ y = A/√2. Option (b) A/2 is the classic trap.

topics.quick_rule: KE = PE at y = A/√2. Remember: √2 not 2 in denominator.

topics.subtopic_name: Time Period of Spring Pendulum — Effect of g

mistakes.label: Spring clock slows on moon
mistakes.description: T = 2π√(m/k) has no g. A spring pendulum clock keeps the same time on moon, on a hill, in a satellite — unlike a simple pendulum.

mistakes.label: Confusing spring pendulum with simple pendulum
mistakes.description: T_simple pendulum = 2π√(l/g) depends on g. T_spring = 2π√(m/k) does NOT depend on g. These two cannot be treated identically.

topics.example: A spring-mass clock on the moon: g_moon = g/6, but T = 2π√(m/k) unchanged. Contrast: simple pendulum clock on moon has T' = √6 × T_Earth and runs 2.45× slower.
topics.trap_framing: Questions give scenario 'on the moon' or 'in a satellite' and ask about time period — the correct answer is 'remains the same' for spring, 'increases' for simple pendulum.

trap_question.stem: A clock based on a spring-mass oscillator is taken to the moon where g is one-sixth of that on Earth. The time period of the spring clock will:
trap_question.trick: T = 2π√(m/k) — g does not appear. Time period remains unchanged. Only simple pendulum (T = 2π√(l/g)) changes with g.

topics.quick_rule: Spring clock: independent of g. Simple pendulum clock: depends on g. On moon, spring clock → same; pendulum clock → runs slower.

mistakes.label: Series spring formula confused with parallel
mistakes.description: Series springs: 1/k_s = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂ (softer, longer T). Parallel springs: k_p = k₁+k₂ (stiffer, shorter T). Students often reverse the two.

mistakes.label: Using resistor analogy directly
mistakes.description: Springs in series behave like resistors in parallel (1/k_s = Σ1/kᵢ). Springs in parallel behave like resistors in series (k_p = Σkᵢ). The analogy is reversed.

topics.example: k₁ = k₂ = k: in series k_s = k/2, T_series = 2π√(2m/k) = √2 × T₀; in parallel k_p = 2k, T_parallel = 2π√(m/2k) = T₀/√2. T_series is always greater.
topics.trap_framing: NEET presents 'compare T_series and T_parallel' — a common wrong answer is T_series < T_parallel (confusing which combination is stiffer).

trap_question.stem: A mass m is attached to two springs of spring constants k₁ and k₂ separately, giving time periods T₁ and T₂. If the springs are connected in series, the time period is:
trap_question.trick: T_series = √(T₁²+T₂²). This is derived from 1/k_s = 1/k₁+1/k₂ and T ∝ 1/√k. Option (c) is T_parallel — the classic confusion.

topics.quick_rule: Series: T = √(T₁²+T₂²). Parallel: T = T₁T₂/√(T₁²+T₂²). Series T is always larger (softer spring).

grounding_summary: Page 711: Chapter 16 opens with periodic vs oscillatory motion definitions; SHM defined as the special case F = −kx; harmonic vs non-harmonic oscillation (y = a sinωt + b sin2ωt); every SHM is periodic but not vice versa.

Page 712: Key definitions — time period, frequency, angular frequency ω = 2πn, phase θ = ωt + φ₀, initial phase/epoch; same phase (Δφ = 2nπ) vs opposite phase (Δφ = (2n+1)π); displacement equations y = a sinωt, y = a cosωt, y = a sin(ωt±φ); SHM as projection of uniform circular motion.

Page 713: Velocity v = aω cosωt = ω√(a²−y²); v_max = aω at y=0; v=0 at y=±a; v-y graph is ellipse (circle for ω=1). Acceleration A = −ω²y; A_max = ω²a at y=±a; A=0 at y=0; A-y graph is straight line, slope = −ω². Comparative table of x, v, a, F at mean and extreme positions.

Page 714: Energy in SHM — PE U = ½ky² = ½mω²y²; KE K = ½mω²(a²−y²); TE E = ½mω²a² = constant. Both KE and PE vary at double the SHM frequency. At y=±a/√2: KE = PE = E/2. At y=±a/2: PE = E/4, KE = 3E/4. Average KE = Average PE = ¼mω²a².

Page 715: Method for finding T — steps (write equilibrium, displace by y, find net restoring force F = −ky, compare with F = −ω²my, get T = 2π√(m/k)). T = 2π√(Inertia factor/Spring factor). Simple pendulum: restoring torque τ = −mgl sinθ ≈ −mglθ; α = −(g/l)θ ⇒ T = 2π√(l/g).

Page 716: Factors affecting simple pendulum period: amplitude (independent for small θ; correction T ≈ T₀[1+θ₀²/16]); mass (independent, illustrated by water-draining hollow sphere); length T ∝ √l; g dependence T ∝ 1/√g with graphs.

Page 717: Pendulum oscillations in liquid (increased T), in electric field (upward E: T increases; downward E: T decreases; horizontal E: g_eff = √(g²+(qE/m)²)); pendulum in lift (4 cases including T=∞ in free fall); accelerated vehicle pendulum; special pendulums — infinite length (T_max ≈ 84.6 min), second's pendulum (T=2s, l≈99.3 cm), compound pendulum T = 2π√(I/mgl).

Page 718: Spring system — Hooke's law F = −kx; k in N/m, [MT⁻²]; k ∝ 1/spring length; spring cut formula k₁ = k(n+1)/n. Spring pendulum T = 2π√(m/k); independent of g. Massive spring m_eff = m + M/3. Reduced mass for two-body system.

Page 719: Spring in vertical vs horizontal plane — same T; equilibrium at l+y₀ for vertical, ky₀ = mg. Series combination: 1/k_s = 1/k₁+1/k₂; T_series = 2π√(m(k₁+k₂)/k₁k₂). Parallel: k_p = k₁+k₂; T_parallel = 2π√(m/(k₁+k₂)). Various SHM formulae: U-tube liquid T = 2π√(L/2g); floating cylinder T = 2π√(l/g); ball in hemispherical bowl T = 2π√((R−r)/g).

Page 720: More SHM formulae — piston in cylinder T = 2π√(Mh/PA); tunnel through Earth chord T = 2π√(R/g) ≈ 84.6 min; torsional pendulum T = 2π√(I/C); longitudinal oscillations of elastic wire T = 2π√(ml/AY). Free, damped, forced and maintained oscillations with descriptions and energy equations.

Page 721: Forced oscillation amplitude formula x₀ = (F₀/m)/√((ω₀²−ω_d²)²+(bω_d/m)²); resonance at ω_d = ω₀; energy resonance; maintained oscillations. Lissajous figures: equation x²/a₁² + y²/a₂² − 2xy cosφ/(a₁a₂) = sin²φ; φ=0°→straight line; φ=90°→ellipse/circle.

Page 722: Tips and Tricks — T_series = √(T₁²+T₂²); T_parallel = T₁T₂/√(T₁²+T₂²); pendulum clock behaviour (slow/fast); infinite series spring k_eff = k/2 (k, 2k, 4k, ...); percentage change in T with l and g; n equal spring parts: each nk, T = T₀/√n; n parts parallel: k_eff = n²k, T = T₀/n. Resultant SHM: y = a sinωt + b cosωt ⇒ A = √(a²+b²). Tension in pendulum string at mean and extreme positions. Objective questions begin with x = A sinωt + B cosωt problem.
grounding_excerpt: Simple harmonic motion is a special type of periodic motion, in which a particle moves to and fro repeatedly about a mean position.
F ∝ - x ⇒ F = - k x
Where k is known as force constant. Its S.I. unit is newton/meter and dimensional formula is [MT^{-2}].
v = dy/dt = aω cos ωt = aω√(1 - sin²ωt) = ω√(a² - y²)
At mean position or equilibrium position (y = 0 and θ = ωt = 0), velocity of particle is maximum and it is v_max = aω.
At extreme position (y = ± a and θ = ωt = π/2), velocity of oscillating particle is zero i.e. v = 0.
A = dv/dt = d/dt(aω cos ωt) = -ω²a sin ωt = -ω²y
In S.H.M. acceleration is maximum at extreme position (at y = ± a). Hence |A_max| = ω²a
E = (1/2)mω²(a² - y²) + (1/2)mω²y² = (1/2)mω²a²
Total energy is not a function of position.
At y = ± a/√2: U = K = E/2
K_average = U_average = (1/2)E = (1/4)mω²a²
T = 2π√(l/g)
The period of simple pendulum is independent of amplitude as long as its motion is simple harmonic.
Time period of simple pendulum is also independent of mass of the bob.
If the lift is moving downward with acceleration a = g, T = 2π√(l/(g-g)) = ∞ and it means there will be no oscillation in a pendulum.
Putting T = 2 s and g = 9.8 m/s² in T = 2π√(l/g) we get l = 0.993 m = 99.3 cm. Hence length of second's pendulum is 99.3 cm or nearly 1 meter on earth surface.
k ∝ 1/Extension = 1/Length of spring. That means if the length of spring is halved then its force constant becomes double.
Time period of a spring pendulum is independent of acceleration due to gravity. That is why a clock based on spring pendulum will keep proper time every where on a hill or moon or in a satellite.
1/k_s = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂ ⇒ k_s = k₁k₂/(k₁+k₂)
k_P = k₁ + k₂
The oscillation of a body whose amplitude goes on decreasing with time is defined as damped oscillation.
Resonance: When the frequency of external force is equal to the natural frequency of the oscillator. Then this state is known as the state of resonance.
If the same body vibrates with series combination of these two springs then for the system time period T = √(T₁² + T₂²)
If the same body vibrates with parallel combination of these two springs then time period of the system T = T₁T₂/√(T₁² + T₂²)

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part II — Chapter 14: Oscillations
sources.why_used: Authoritative NEET syllabus reference for SHM — cross-checked definitions of SHM, time period formulae for spring pendulum (T = 2π√(m/k)) and simple pendulum (T = 2π√(l/g)), energy equations, and resonance description.

sources.title: HyperPhysics — Simple Harmonic Motion
sources.why_used: Verified velocity v = ω√(A²−x²), acceleration a = −ω²x, energy expressions KE = ½mω²(A²−x²) and PE = ½mω²x², and the phase relationships between displacement, velocity and acceleration in SHM.

key_facts: SHM defining condition: F = −kx; force constant k in N/m with dimensional formula [MT⁻²]
Every SHM is periodic, but every periodic motion is not SHM
Displacement: x = a sinωt (from mean); x = a cosωt (from extreme); general form x = a sin(ωt ± φ)
Velocity: v = ω√(a²−x²); v_max = aω at x = 0; v = 0 at x = ±a
Acceleration: a = −ω²x; a_max = ω²A at x = ±A; a = 0 at x = 0; graph A vs x is straight line slope −ω²
v-x graph is an ellipse: v²/(aω)² + x²/a² = 1 (circle when ω = 1)
Velocity leads displacement by π/2; acceleration leads velocity by π/2; acceleration is π ahead of displacement
Total Energy E = ½mω²a² = constant (independent of position, proportional to a²)
KE = PE at displacement y = A/√2 (NOT A/2)
Average KE = Average PE = E/2 = ¼mω²a²; both KE and PE vary at frequency 2ω
Simple pendulum T = 2π√(l/g); independent of mass and amplitude (small oscillations)
Second's pendulum: T = 2 s, l ≈ 99.3 cm on Earth
Pendulum free-fall: T = ∞ (no oscillation); accelerating upward: T decreases; accelerating downward: T increases
Spring T = 2π√(m/k); independent of g — spring clock keeps time on moon/satellite
k ∝ 1/length; spring halved → k doubled. Spring cut into n equal parts: each part has constant nk
Series springs: 1/k_s = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂; T_series = √(T₁²+T₂²)
Parallel springs: k_p = k₁+k₂; T_parallel = T₁T₂/√(T₁²+T₂²); T_series > T_parallel
Massive spring m_eff = m + M/3; two-body system: reduced mass 1/m_r = 1/m₁ + 1/m₂
Resonance: ω_d = ω₀; amplitude and power absorption maximum; velocity in phase with driving force
Lissajous figures: two perpendicular SHMs; φ=0 → straight line; φ=90°, a₁=a₂ → circle; general → ellipse

review.notes: All content sourced exclusively from OCR pages 711–722 of physics-1. Verbatim excerpts used in grounding_excerpt array. No external research required — textbook coverage is comprehensive. NEET exam scope confirmed: SHM is a high-yield chapter (3-4 questions per paper). Key trap topics identified from common error patterns visible in textbook tips-and-tricks section (page 722).
